title: Error Handling in a Server
concept: Returning structured errors instead of crashing, so the host can gracefully recover or inform the user.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we exposed Resources from our MCP server. What is the primary purpose of a resource?

options.text: They let the client execute arbitrary shell commands.
They expose read-only data, like database records or files, via URI patterns.

exercises.explain: In the prior lesson, we learned that MCP resources act like read-only endpoints (like URLs) that expose structured data or files to the client.

cards.title: When Things Go Wrong
cards.body: When a tool fails in a database or a file is missing, a bad server crashes and terminates the connection. A good server replies with a structured error, letting the AI try a different strategy.

cards.title: Structured Error Responses
cards.body: Error handling in an MCP server involves catching issues like missing tools or offline databases, and returning standard JSON-RPC error responses. This keeps the server running and tells the LLM exactly what went wrong.

cards.title: The Polite Waiter
cards.body: Imagine a waiter at a restaurant. If they run out of soup, they don't faint on the floor; they politely tell you it's unavailable so you can order something else. In MCP, your server should act like that waiter, returning a clear message instead of crashing.

cards.title: A Clean Refusal
cards.body: If a tool asks to read '/secret.txt' and gets a permission error, the server responds with code -32602 (Invalid Params) and a message 'File access denied', allowing the LLM client to explain the block to the user.

cards.title: Crashing is Not Error Handling
cards.body: You might think that throwing a standard code error (like throw new Error()) is enough. However, unless you catch it and format it into a JSON-RPC error response, the host application will just see a disconnected stream and fail.

cards.title: Crash vs. Handle
cards.body: Compare a server that crashes when a resource is missing versus one that handles the error gracefully.

left.text: Process exits immediately. Connection closed, causing the host application to fail.

right.text: Sends error code -32000. Connection stays open, allowing the LLM to recover.

exercises.prompt: What happens if your MCP server experiences an unhandled exception and crashes instead of returning an error message?

options.text: The host application will automatically fix the server code.
The connection terminates abruptly, causing the LLM session to fail.
The server automatically ignores the error and retries the task.
The user is shown a clean message explaining the missing database.

exercises.explain: If the server crashes, the entire connection dies and the host application (or LLM) cannot continue the session gracefully.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Sending a clear error message instead of crashing allows the LLM to try a different approach or inform the user.
exercises.explain: Returning structured errors allows the LLM to learn why the tool failed (e.g., 'File not found') and adapt, perhaps by asking the user for a different file name.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept about how MCP servers communicate issues:
exercises.sentence: To prevent the host application from crashing, our server must send a structured JSON-RPC error response back to the client.
exercises.explain: Instead of shutting down, the server packages the error into a JSON-RPC error response, which preserves the connection and informs the caller.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following represents a structured error response that an MCP server should send back to a client?

options.text: SystemError: DB_OFFLINE at server.js:42:11 (Process terminated with code 1)
{ "result": { "data": "Something failed, but we are not sure what." } }

exercises.explain: Option B represents a structured, standard error response format that allows the calling client to handle the issue programmatically without crashing. Option A is a raw system trace, and Option C is an empty mock response.

exercises.prompt: When an MCP server encounters an unsupported tool call or a missing database connection, what is the best way to handle it to ensure a resilient system?

options.text: Throwing a generic language exception so the server immediately restarts.
Returning a JSON-RPC error response with a specific error code and message.
Ignoring the error completely and returning empty data.
Shutting down the host application using an emergency system exit.

exercises.explain: Returning an error code like METHOD_NOT_FOUND or INVALID_PARAMS inside a JSON-RPC response allows the LLM client to understand what went wrong and try a different approach, rather than crashing the entire session.
